teaching.title: Java for Loop
teaching.analogy: A for loop is a treadmill with a timer — you set speed (init), check time remaining (condition), and update after each step. All three parts are visible in one line.
teaching.content: for (init; condition; update) { body }. Init runs once. Condition checked before each iteration. Update runs after each iteration. Loop variable declared in init is scoped to the loop.

examples.title: Classic for Loop
Accumulator Pattern
examples.explanation: Declare sum OUTSIDE the loop to persist.

teaching.funFact: Java's for loop was inherited directly from C (1972) — unchanged for 50+ years. for (init; condition; update) is the same in C, C++, Java, and JavaScript! 🏛️

teaching.title: Enhanced for-each Loop
teaching.analogy: for-each is a waiter serving every table in order — no counting, no tracking indices. Just: give me each item one by one.
teaching.content: for (Type item : collection) — iterates every element. Simpler than index loops. Works on arrays and any Iterable. Cannot use/modify the index during iteration.

examples.title: for-each with Arrays

teaching.funFact: Java added for-each in Java 5 (2004). It uses the Iterator pattern under the hood — no off-by-one errors possible since you never manage an index! ✅

mcq.q: for(int i=0; i<5; i++) — how many iterations?
mcq.explanation: Count: i = 0,1,2,3,4 → 5 iterations. Pattern: 0 to <N gives exactly N iterations.

mcq.q: Can you access 'i' after the for loop ends if declared in the init?
options: No — i is scoped to the loop
mcq.explanation: Scope: int i declared in for(int i=...) only lives inside the loop block. Accessing it after causes a compile error. Declare before the for if you need the final value.

mcq.q: for(int x : arr) — what does x represent?
mcq.explanation: For-Each: x is the VALUE of each element in turn — not the index. If you need the index, use a traditional for loop with arr[i].

mcq.q: int sum=0; for(int i=1;i<=4;i++) sum+=i; What is sum?
What is wrong: for(int i=0; i<=arr.length; i++) with arr of 3 elements?
mcq.explanation: Off-by-One: arr has indices 0,1,2. arr.length=3. arr[3] throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException! Always use i < arr.length (strict <).

coding.title: Loop iterations
coding.starterCode: // for(int i=0; i<7; i++) runs ___ times

coding.title: Sum 1 to 10
coding.objective: int sum=0; for(int i=1;i<=10;i++) sum+=i; Output?

coding.title: for-each colon
coding.objective: What separator goes between variable and array in for-each?
coding.hint: for (Type var : collection) — colon separates them.

options: i-- decrements — i goes negative, stays <5 forever. Change to i++
debugging.answer: i-- decrements — i goes negative, stays <5 forever. Change to i++
debugging.hint: i-- makes i smaller. It will always be < 5 → infinite loop.

options: i<=arr.length accesses arr[3] which doesn't exist. Use i < arr.length
debugging.answer: i<=arr.length accesses arr[3] which doesn't exist. Use i < arr.length

teaching.title: while Loop
teaching.analogy: while is a guard checking tickets BEFORE entry. If nobody has a ticket from the start, nobody gets in. The check happens BEFORE every attempt.
teaching.content: while (condition) { body } — checks condition BEFORE each iteration. If false from the start, body never runs. Always update the loop variable inside or risk infinite loop.

examples.title: while Loop

teaching.funFact: while is the fundamental loop in computer science theory. for loops are syntactic sugar for while. Any for can be rewritten as a while! 🔬

teaching.title: do-while Loop
teaching.analogy: do-while insists you taste the food before deciding to order more. You ALWAYS get the first serving, THEN decide if you want another round.
teaching.content: do { body } while (condition); — body runs ONCE first, then checks condition. Note the semicolon after the closing parenthesis! Perfect for input validation.

examples.title: do-while
examples.explanation: Runs at least once — semicolon required.

teaching.funFact: do-while is the least-used Java loop but shines for input validation — the 'ask at least once, keep asking until valid' pattern is perfect for it! 🎮

mcq.q: What is the key difference between while and do-while?
options: do-while always runs at least once; while may never run
mcq.explanation: Key Difference: while checks FIRST — if false, never runs. do-while runs FIRST then checks — guarantees at least one execution.

mcq.q: What is REQUIRED at the end of do { } while (cond)?
mcq.explanation: Semicolon: do { } while (condition); needs a semicolon after the closing ). A very common Java syntax error to forget it!

mcq.q: int x=10; while(x<5){x++;} How many times does body run?
mcq.explanation: Zero: x=10, x<5 is false immediately. while checks BEFORE running — body never executes.

mcq.q: Best loop for 'keep asking until valid input'?
mcq.explanation: Input Validation: do-while — always ask at least once, keep asking while invalid. Perfect match for the 'run once then check' pattern.

coding.title: while last value
coding.hint: i goes 1,2,3 then 4<=3 false. Last print: 3.

coding.title: do-while punctuation
coding.objective: What ends the do-while condition line?
coding.hint: do { } while(condition); — semicolon after ) is mandatory!

options: No i++ inside — i never changes, i<=5 always true. Add i++; in body.
debugging.answer: No i++ inside — i never changes, i<=5 always true. Add i++; in body.
debugging.hint: Always update the loop variable inside while! Without i++, the condition never becomes false.

teaching.title: break & continue
teaching.analogy: break is an emergency exit — everyone leaves immediately. continue is 'next please!' — skip this person, serve the next one. break exits; continue skips one iteration.
teaching.content: break: exits the current loop entirely. continue: skips rest of current iteration, moves to next (for: runs update; while: re-checks condition). Both only affect the INNERMOST loop.

examples.title: break and continue
Nested Loops
examples.explanation: break only exits the INNER loop.

teaching.funFact: Java supports labeled break to exit OUTER loops: 'outer: for(...) { for(...) { break outer; } }' — unique to Java! C and C++ don't have labeled breaks. 🏷️

mcq.q: What does break do inside a loop?
mcq.explanation: break: Immediately exits the CURRENT loop. Execution continues with the first statement AFTER the loop's closing }.

mcq.q: What does continue do in a for loop?
options: Skips current iteration, runs update, re-checks
mcq.explanation: continue: Skips rest of CURRENT iteration. In for: runs update (i++) then condition. Loop doesn't exit — just skips one turn.

mcq.q: In nested loops, which loop does break exit?
mcq.explanation: Innermost Only: break exits the innermost loop it's in. Use Java labeled break to exit outer loops.
continue: When i=3, skip print. Output: 0 1 2 (skip 3) 4 → '0 1 2 4'.

coding.title: break exit value
coding.hint: Prints 0,1,2,3. At i=4 → break. Last print: 3.

coding.title: continue skip
coding.hint: When i=3, continue skips that print.

options: break before println — exits on first iteration, nothing prints. Put break inside a condition.
debugging.answer: break before println — exits on first iteration, nothing prints. Put break inside a condition.
debugging.hint: Unconditional break at top exits immediately. break should be inside an if condition.

teaching.title: ⚠️ BOSS — THE ITERATOR
teaching.analogy: The Iterator runs every loop in the JVM simultaneously. Prove you can trace any loop, predict any output, fix any infinite loop.
teaching.content: Tests ALL Level 3 Java: for anatomy, loop scope, for-each, while vs do-while, break/continue, nested loops, off-by-one errors.

examples.title: Full Loop Arsenal
examples.code:
✅ for (init; condition; update)
✅ for (Type x : arr) — for-each
✅ while — check first
✅ do { } while(); — run first, semicolon!
✅ break — exit loop
✅ continue — skip iteration
✅ Nested loops — break exits innermost
✅ i < arr.length (not <=)

teaching.funFact: Java 8 streams are a functional alternative to loops: list.stream().filter(x->x>5).forEach(System.out::println). But loops are clearer for beginners and faster in most benchmarks! 🌊

mcq.q: for(int i=2;i<11;i+=3) — what are the values of i?
int[] a={10,20,30}. for(int x:a) print(x+1). Output?
Which loop guarantees at least one execution?
mcq.explanation: do-while: runs body FIRST, checks after. Even if condition is false initially, runs once.

mcq.q: Nested: for(i<3) for(j<4) print(). Total runs?

options: Two bugs: i=1 skips d[0] AND i<=length causes ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException. Fix: i=0, i<d.length
debugging.answer: Two bugs: i=1 skips d[0] AND i<=length causes ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException. Fix: i=0, i<d.length
debugging.hint: Arrays are 0-indexed. i=0 and i < arr.length — always!
